nativeRange.habitat: Cryptic species of seaward reef slopes; found among patches of rubble or among branching coral, generally from around 6 m down to at least 40 m. Rare in shallower water.

decorPreferences: abundant live rock with caves and crevices
fine sand substrate at least 5 cm deep

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis, frozen brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty foods, and high-quality marine flake or pellet formulas for carnivores. Has been documented foraging actively on copepods, amphipods, pyramidellid snails, commensal flatworms, and bristleworms from live rock, so a mature system with a healthy pod population is commonly considered beneficial.

avoidWith: fairy and flasher wrasses introduced after it
very shy fish such as firefish and pipefish

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 3268x2179. Tack-sharp in-situ shot; deep scarlet body with fine pale pinstripes, yellow snout, white/blue cheek streak and red eye all clearly shown; fills frame.
Roger Rittmaster, 1523x1218. Sharp clean lateral profile; fine pinstripes, white cheek stripe and yellow snout evident on a vibrant individual; good angle variety from a second source.
Diego Delso, 3503x2335. Sharp in-situ portrait of a second individual; scarlet-pink body with fine pinstripes and diagnostic cheek streak; slightly softer background bokeh.

summary: The Scarlet Pin Stripe Wrasse is a small, secretive Indo-Pacific reef fish with deep scarlet coloration, fine pale pinstripes, and a pale blue streak across the cheek. It is generally considered reef-safe and is widely kept as a natural predator of pyramidellid snails, flatworms, and bristleworms.
careNotes:
The Scarlet Pin Stripe Wrasse (Pseudocheilinus evanidus) is a cryptic Indo-Pacific reef fish that tends to top out around 7.5–9 cm. It is generally considered reef-safe with corals and most ornamental invertebrates, though it has been reported to hunt small shrimp and is commonly used as a natural control for pyramidellid snails (parasitic snails that prey on corals), commensal flatworms, and bristleworms.

Despite its small size, around 200 L (roughly 55 US gallons) is generally advised. This reflects the species' semi-aggressive temperament toward smaller wrasses and its need for an extensive rockwork network with multiple retreat routes; smaller tanks tend to amplify territorial pressure on tankmates.

Water parameters should be kept in the 22–27°C range, with pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, and specific gravity 1.020–1.025.

This species is described as secretive on introduction and tends to disappear into the rockwork for days to weeks before establishing routine patrols. A fine-sand substrate at least 5 cm deep is generally recommended; the fish has been documented burying itself in the sand to sleep, as is common among Pseudocheilinus wrasses.

P. evanidus is widely described as semi-aggressive, particularly toward peaceful wrasses, other Pseudocheilinus, and similarly-sized cryptic fish introduced after it. The standard advice is to add it LAST, after more timid tankmates have established themselves. It generally holds its own against tangs, angelfish, and larger clownfish.

Like most wrasses, it is an able jumper, so a tightly fitting lid or mesh cover with no large openings is essential. It is widely believed to be a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite — meaning individuals are born female and may change to male later in life — as is typical for the wrasse family.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of this species has not been reliably documented in available aquarium sources, and spawning in home aquaria is generally considered unlikely outside of dedicated breeder setups.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page reached via search after the wrasse-reef-safe collection landing page did not list this species. Provided max size (3 in), minimum tank (55 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (Moderate), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), reef-safe (Yes), carnivorous diet, and notes on secretive behavior, jumping risk, and the recommendation to add last.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Labridae / Eupercaria misc). Max length 9.0 cm TL. Distribution: Indo-Pacific from Red Sea to South Africa, east to Hawaiian and Tuamotu Islands, north to Izu Islands. Habitat: rubble patches and branching coral on seaward reef slopes, 6–40+ m. Wild temperature 23–28°C. Generally solitary; feeds on small benthic invertebrates. Oviparous, distinct pairing during breeding. IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3 in), minimum tank (24 gal — significantly lower than LiveAquaria), temperature (72–79°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe (yes with caveat about prized small shrimp), moderate care level, Indo-Pacific range (Indonesia to Hawaii), and documented natural predation on pyramidellid snails, flatworms, and bristleworms.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (76–80°F → 24–27°C), pH (7.8–8.4), SG (1.023–1.026), dKH (8–12). Confirmed jumping risk and need for tight-fitting canopy, secretive nature, requirement for live-rock crevices and ~5 cm sandbed, and aggression toward conspecifics.

additional.name: FishyWiki
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (7.6 cm), minimum tank (207 L / ~55 gal — aligns with LiveAquaria), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, carnivorous diet, semi-aggressive temperament (especially toward peaceful wrasses), reef-safe, moderate care. The designated Stage A secondary (Reef2Reef wiki thread) returned HTTP 403 so this source was used in its place.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive-breeding accounts found in available hobby or scientific sources. FishBase notes distinct pairing during breeding in the wild.
